evidence_quotes.quote: Academic Integrity Include your college's Academic Integrity; it is likely AP 5500: Cheating, plagiarism (including plagiarism in a student publication), or engaging in other academic dishonesty. For purposes of this provision, the term 'cheating' includes, but is not limited to: Use of any unauthorized assistance in taking quizzes, tests, or exams; Use of sources beyond those authorized by the instructor in writing papers, preparing reports, solving problems, or carrying out other assignments; The acquisition, without permission, of tests or other academic material belonging to the College. The term 'plagiarism' includes, but is not limited to, the use, by paraphrase or direct quotation, of the published or unpublished work of another person without full and clear acknowledgment. It also includes the unacknowledged use of materials prepared by another person or entity engaged in the writing and/or selling of term papers or other academic materials.

evidence_quotes.quote: Welcome to the first week of our Public Speaking class! This week is split between our course orientation and learning about some core foundations regarding the concept of public speaking, its evolution, and how that dynamic looks different in a virtual space as opposed to your standard face-to-face environment. Our specific objectives for this week include the following: Identify and describe key models of communication (transactional & interactional) Distinguish between public speaking best practices in a virtual versus on site environment Identify and describe rhetorical proofs, as well as rhetorical canons Compare diverse global perspectives on Public Speaking.

Welcome to the second week of the course! Here's what we're aiming to accomplish this week: Develop & deliver an extemporaneous speech in a virtual environment. Identify and describe the types and root causes of communication apprehension. Create an anxiety management plan to address communication apprehension during the course and beyond. Distinguish between the different modes of speech delivery (extemporaneous, impromptu, manuscript, etc.)

evidence_quotes.quote: Speech #2 Self-Reflection: Core Values Presentation Although we learn a great deal by simply doing, it's just as important to reflect on our performance after a presentation. This assignment gives you the opportunity to critically evaluate your strengths and areas for improvement following your Core Values Speech. If you recorded your speech, reviewing it before writing your reflection will make this process much more insightful. Reflection Prompts Your self-reflection should address both your performance and your process. Use the guiding questions below to help you develop a thoughtful and detailed response. Performance & Improvement What do you feel were your specific strengths during the presentation? Why? What specific areas of weakness may have affected the effectiveness of your speech? Why? What concrete steps can you take to improve your next presentation? Process Preparation: How did you prepare for this speech? Do you feel your preparation was effective? Content: You know your material well, but your audience only saw a portion of it. Did you need to cut any parts that may have impacted your message or clarity? Limitations: What challenges did you face (e.g., time, nerves, environment) that may have limited your ability to present the way you intended?

The reflections are clearly connected to learning goals and personal growth in public speaking.
